scenario_results.reason: Retention-schedule guidance stayed bounded, traced recommendations to authorities, and requested missing ownership details rather than guessing.
File-plan review surfaced traceability gaps and ambiguous triggers without pretending coverage was complete.
Disposition guidance required evidence, approval, and sanitization controls before allowing defensible destruction.
Regulated-retention mapping used official SEC and FINRA sources carefully and escalated legal interpretation instead of overreaching.
Microsoft capability review used official product and licensing sources to explain retention constraints without drifting into procurement or implementation work.
Google Workspace plan review stayed evidence-based, identified retention-tier limits, and avoided budget or licensing ownership.
Remediation sequencing ranked work by exposure, evidence weakness, and disposal risk rather than convenience.
Conflicting-jurisdiction case was escalated with explicit uncertainty and minimum missing facts instead of unsupported harmonization.
Legal-retention interpretation was rejected clearly with correct routing to legal or compliance ownership.
Litigation-hold administration was rejected as out of scope while preserving narrow records-retention context.
Platform configuration work was routed out of scope without losing the approved retention-guidance baseline.
Fallback handling preserved the local reviewed baseline and avoided claims beyond specialist-owned evidence when delegated meta-agent outputs were stale.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against issue #133 requirements, including policy and controls analysis, commercial and licensing constraint reasoning, evidence traceability, risk-based prioritization, low-confidence escalation, out-of-scope rejection, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification is recorded in readiness evidence.
